angle.headline: Why Your M&A Diligence Process Finds Everything Except What Kills Deals
angle.thesis: Middle-market owners spend six figures on diligence that audits the past but never diagnoses whether the target can operate without its founder — the single variable that determines post-close performance.
angle.targetPersona: Business owners in growth-through-acquisition mode, particularly those who've completed one deal and are now considering their second, operating with $10M-$75M revenue across the US-LATAM corridor
angle.relevanceToAudience: With USMCA at its six-year review and nearshoring momentum creating acquisition opportunities across the corridor, owners are evaluating targets in Mexico and LATAM — but standard diligence frameworks were built for US-only deals and miss the founder-dependency risk that is magnified across borders and jurisdictions.
talkingPoints: Most M&A diligence answers 'Does this business make money?' but never asks 'Can this business make decisions without the seller in the room?' — the real post-close question.

The failure happens because diligence is structured to audit the past (financials, compliance, IP) rather than diagnose the operating model's ability to execute independently — particularly across time zones and legal systems in corridor deals.

The Machine Test: before you sign the LOI, spend one week asking only this: 'Who makes what decisions when the owner is unavailable?' If the answer is 'it waits' or 'it escalates,' you are buying a job, not a business.
Add one diligence workstream focused exclusively on decision rights, escalation patterns, and information flow — not as compliance check, but as operating model architecture assessment.

peopleAngle.challenge: The seller's leadership team has never operated without the founder's real-time input, and the buyer's integration team lacks the trust or context to make decisions during the 90-day transition window when founder dependency becomes operationally visible.
peopleAngle.framework: Kotter Step 2: Build the Guiding Coalition

entries.fieldReportSummary: [Meta announced this week](https://www.businessinsider.com/meta-layoffs-internal-memo-ai-job-cuts-faq-2026-5) that it will cut 10% of staff on Wednesday, eliminating around 8,000 roles in three waves of notifications timed to 4am local time across regions. angle.headline: Your Innovation Team Is Theater. Your Line Employees Are the Real R&D.
angle.thesis: Most middle-market companies fail at innovation not because they lack ideas, but because they built innovation as a separate function instead of embedding problem-solving authority into the people who actually do the work.
angle.targetPersona: Founder-CEOs with $10M-$75M revenue who created an 'innovation committee' or hired a Chief Innovation Officer but still see no meaningful operational improvement
angle.relevanceToAudience: US-LATAM corridor operators have built businesses on relationship and instinct, but when they try to formalize innovation, they copy Fortune 500 structures that don't work at their scale—creating overhead without velocity, and alienating the frontline employees who already know what's broken.
talkingPoints: CEOs say growth depends on innovation but 80%+ of revenue still comes from the same products they offered five years ago—the innovation theater isn't producing results.
The dysfunction happens because innovation gets assigned to people furthest from customer pain, while the employees closest to problems are never given authority to solve them.
The framework is decision rights architecture: map who encounters which problems, then push solve authority to that level with clear constraints and fast feedback loops.

The action is to kill your innovation committee this quarter and instead give three frontline teams a $10K budget and 90 days to fix the one thing that wastes their time most—then showcase the winner company-wide.

peopleAngle.challenge: Frontline employees have been trained to escalate problems upward, not solve them—rewiring them to own solutions requires explicit permission, skill development, and visible protection when experiments fail.
